# Budget Briefing for Jess (Anxious Pre-K Parent)

**What the FY27 budget means for families entering South Portland schools**

---

## The Short Version

South Portland schools are going through major changes right now. The district is cutting 78 staff positions, closing one elementary school, and reorganizing how the remaining schools work. If your child enters kindergarten in fall 2026 or later, they'll enter a different system than what exists today. Here's what you need to know.

## Will Full-Day Kindergarten Still Exist?

Yes. Full-day kindergarten is not on the chopping block. The discussion is about **which buildings** serve which grades, not about eliminating grade levels.

## Which School Will My Child Attend?

That depends on what the board decides in the next few weeks.

The current proposal **closes Dyer Elementary** and redistricts its students. If you live in the Dyer zone, your child would be assigned to a different school. The four remaining schools (Brown, Kaler, Small, Skillin) would continue operating.

An earlier proposal would have gone further — converting some schools to "primary" campuses (pre-K through grade 1) and others to "intermediate" campuses (grades 2-4). That model is still on the table as an option for future years, but the March 9 budget proposal is simpler: close Dyer, keep the others as pre-K through 4.

**Bottom line:** Your neighborhood school assignment may change. Watch for redistricting maps — they haven't been published yet.

## What About Pre-K?

A new state mandate (CDS transition) is bringing **80-90 four-year-olds** with special education needs into the district's responsibility starting September 2026, with three-year-olds following in FY28. The district is joining "CDS Cohort 3" to prepare.

This is happening regardless of the budget — it's a state law requirement. But it means the district is absorbing new young children at the same time it's cutting staff and closing a school. How well that transition goes is an open question.

The district is also exploring a **Head Start partnership** (details expected in March) and expanding its **Community School model**, which provides wraparound services (health center, food distribution, parent engagement). The SPMS Community School has shown strong results — chronic absenteeism dropped from 22% to 10.5%.

## Is the District Investing or Declining?

Honestly, it's managing decline in enrollment while trying to maintain quality. Enrollment has fallen 11% since 2015-16, and the student body has become significantly more diverse and complex (17% multilingual learners, 23% special education, 10% experiencing homelessness). The district has more expensive needs with fewer students to spread costs across.

The **fund balance is gone** — no savings cushion. State funding covers only 20% of actual costs. The budget gap is $8.4M.

That said, the Community School strategy and the pre-K expansion are forward-looking investments. The question is whether the district can sustain them while cutting 12% of its workforce.

## What to Watch For

- **Redistricting maps:** Which neighborhoods feed into which schools after Dyer closes
- **Pre-K programming details:** Head Start partnership, CDS transition plan
- **Class sizes at the elementary level:** 42 fewer teachers means larger classes somewhere
- **The June 9 referendum:** If the budget fails the public vote, the district must cut further

## How to Get Involved

You don't have to attend every meeting. But two are worth your time:

- **March 16 (6 PM, SPHS):** Open conversation with district leadership — you can ask questions directly
- **June 9:** Vote in the budget referendum

If you can't attend, submit questions at spsdme.org/budget27. And ask other parents in your playgroup or daycare what they've heard — you're not the only one trying to figure this out.

---

*Based on all public budget documents and meeting proceedings through March 10, 2026.*
